prompt: Implement a production-quality React 18 + TypeScript autocomplete component. Export it as the default function with props { searchUsers }, where searchUsers(query: string, signal: AbortSignal): Promise<Array<{id:string,name:string}>>. Keep the query input controlled by React state. Debounce nonempty queries by exactly 250 ms, cancel pending and in-flight obsolete work with AbortController, and ensure stale responses never replace newer results even if a search implementation ignores abort. Support ArrowDown/ArrowUp navigation with wrapping, Enter selection, Escape dismissal, and mouse selection. Selecting a user puts the name in the input and closes the popup without starting another search. Provide correct combobox/listbox/option ARIA relationships and expose loading, empty, and error feedback accessibly. Empty input performs no search. Use React only; no external component or debounce packages. Return one fenced tsx implementation with no tests or main routine, followed by a concise explanation of races, cleanup, accessibility, and deterministic timer/interaction tests. Keep the answer under 1400 words.

Implement an idempotent POST /orders endpoint in TypeScript using Express and PostgreSQL. Return exactly two files in manifest order. Install authenticate before the JSON parser; it supplies req.authenticatedUserId as a positive decimal string or sends its own 401 response. Capture the exact raw JSON bytes as req.rawBody before parsing. Accept only POST /orders with a nonempty trimmed Idempotency-Key of at most 128 characters and an exact JSON object {sku, quantity}: sku is a trimmed 1..100-character string and quantity is an integer 1..1000. Invalid key or body returns 400. Hash the exact raw bytes with SHA-256. For a successful first request return 201 and {id:string,sku:string,quantity:number}. Concurrent requests with the same user, key, and exact bytes must create one order and return that same 201 payload; the same key with different bytes returns 409 {error:"idempotency_key_reused"}. A matching processing record returns 409 {error:"request_in_progress"}. Different users may reuse a key independently. Use a database transaction plus a unique database constraint and row locking or an equivalent database-only protocol; do not use process-local locks. On a database failure return 500 without leaving an order or idempotency record committed. Second, a heading ### `schema.sql` followed immediately by one fenced code block whose fence label is exactly `sql`. Create public.orders with id, user_id, sku, quantity, and created_at, and public.order_idempotency with user_id, idempotency_key, request_sha256, state, order_id, response_status, response_body, created_at, and completed_at. Enforce a primary or unique key on (user_id, idempotency_key), valid processing/completed state transitions, and an order reference for completed records. Do not create a server listener or tests. Follow the files with concise prose explaining the transaction race protocol and failure handling. Do not include other fenced blocks. Keep the answer under 1700 words.

Implement the freestanding C11 API declared by i2c_controller.h for a fictional I2C0 controller on a single-core little-endian Cortex-M3 (ARMv7-M, AAPCS/EABI). Include i2c_controller.h. Valid foreground poll, prepare_sleep, resume, and is_pressed calls save-disable exactly once and restore the exact state; invalid/uninitialized poll and is_pressed calls have no GPIO or interrupt-mask side effect. poll returns NONE without reading input while sleeping, inactive, or before `(uint32_t)(now_ms - candidate_started_at_ms) >= GPIO_DEBOUNCE_INTERVAL_MS`. POST /jobs accepts exactly one JSON object with a nonempty id; return 202 only after enqueueing, 429 when the bounded queue is full, 503 once shutdown admission closes, 405 for other methods, 415 for invalid media types, and 400 for invalid bodies. Exactly four workers call Processor. Report processor errors and panics to OnError without terminating a worker. Shutdown must reject new work before closing the queue, stop the HTTP server, finish every accepted job, avoid send-on-closed-channel races, and return the caller context error on deadline expiry; deadline expiry must cancel processor contexts. RunContext must use the configured positive ShutdownTimeout or the fixed ten-second default after cancellation; negative durations are invalid. Run converts SIGINT/SIGTERM into cancellation. Return exactly two files in manifest order: a heading ### `server.go` immediately followed by one fenced Go block containing the implementation, then a heading ### `server_test.go` immediately followed by one fenced Go block containing focused submit/shutdown-race and deadline tests. Both files must declare package shutdown. Do not include other fenced blocks. Follow the files with a concise prose explanation of shutdown ordering, queue-full behavior, and error handling. Keep the answer under 1600 words.

Implement pub struct StreamDecoder in stable Rust without external crates for the supplied crate::decoder_api API. The Decoder trait requires fn new() -> Self, fn push(&mut self, chunk: &[u8]) -> Vec<Vec<u8>>, and fn buffered_len(&self) -> usize; MAX_PAYLOAD is 1 MiB. Begin with use crate::decoder_api::{Decoder, MAX_PAYLOAD}; and implement Decoder for StreamDecoder. Frames contain magic bytes 0xCA 0xFE, a u32 big-endian payload length, payload bytes, and one trailing XOR checksum over the payload. Input arrives in arbitrary chunks and may contain garbage before a valid frame. Cap payloads at MAX_PAYLOAD; buffered_len must report retained bytes and never exceed MAX_PAYLOAD + 7. Avoid panics and unbounded buffering; recover after bad magic, oversized lengths, bad checksums, and truncated chunks; emit zero or more owned payloads per push; process multiple frames; and preserve a possible trailing 0xCA at chunk boundaries. Return one fenced Rust implementation that does not define main or tests, followed by a concise explanation of resynchronization, complexity, memory bounds, and focused tests. Keep the answer under 1500 words.

Implement a generic TypeScript singleflight cache as one self-contained module. Export type CacheLoader<K, V> = (key: K) => V | PromiseLike<V>, interface SingleflightCacheOptions with ttlMs: number and optional now: () => number, and class SingleflightCache<K, V>. The constructor accepts SingleflightCacheOptions and rejects negative, non-finite TTLs with RangeError. Expose get(key: K, loader: CacheLoader<K, V>, signal?: AbortSignal): Promise<V>, invalidate(key: K): void, and clear(): void. Successful values remain fresh while now() is strictly less than their expiry; ttlMs 0 disables reuse. At most one current loader runs per key, including during synchronous loader execution. A caller may reject its own wait with signal.reason without cancelling the shared load needed by others, and an already-aborted caller must not invoke or join a loader. Loader rejections and synchronous throws are returned as promise rejections and are not cached. invalidate(key) removes a cached value, detaches the current in-flight load, and prevents that older load from repopulating or overwriting newer data; clear() provides the same guarantee for every key. Expiry must be lazy with no per-entry timers. Use no external packages. Return one fenced TypeScript implementation with no tests or main routine, followed by a concise explanation of race handling, complexity, memory bounds, and deterministic fake-clock tests. Keep the answer under 1600 words.

Implement the declared two-file TypeScript/SQL webhook fixture. Return exactly these sections in this order, with no additional fenced code: ### `handler.ts` followed immediately by a fence; its fence label is exactly `typescript`, then ### `schema.sql` followed immediately by a fence; its fence label is exactly `sql`. In handler.ts, export createWebhookApp(pool: Pool, secrets: readonly string[], now?: () => number): Express. It must register POST /webhook and use only Express, pg, and Node built-ins. now returns Unix milliseconds and defaults to Date.now. The sender supplies X-Webhook-Id (a trimmed 1–128 character value), X-Webhook-Timestamp (a safe decimal Unix-seconds integer), and X-Webhook-Signature (exactly 64 lowercase hexadecimal characters). Verify HMAC-SHA256 over UTF-8 timestamp + '.' followed by the exact raw request bytes, using constant-time comparison, before JSON parsing. Accept any nonempty configured secret to support rotation. Reject timestamps more than five minutes from now. Parse only an application/json raw body of at most 16 KiB and require a JSON object. Return 400 {"error":"invalid_request"} for malformed headers, 401 {"error":"invalid_signature"} for an invalid HMAC, 400 {"error":"timestamp_out_of_range"} for a stale timestamp, and 400 {"error":"invalid_body"} after authenticated invalid JSON. schema.sql must create public.webhook_events with webhook_id as its primary key plus authenticated_sha256, signed_timestamp, and payload fields, and public.webhook_outbox with a unique webhook_id foreign key, payload, and topic constrained to webhook.received. In one PostgreSQL transaction, claim the webhook ID, persist the event, and enqueue exactly one outbox row. Bind the ID to a SHA-256 digest of the signed timestamp plus exact body bytes; a duplicate with the same digest returns the same 202 {"status":"accepted"} response without a second outbox row, while a valid delivery with changed authenticated content returns 409 {"error":"webhook_id_reused"}. Roll back both rows and return 500 {"error":"internal_error"} on database failure. Keep the answer under 1700 words.
